courses.title: Build Something Real
courses.description: Hands-on ChatGPT tutorial: build real tools, automate workflows, and create your first AI-powered system step by step.

lessons.title: Pick Your Project
lessons.description: Choose what you're building: a Custom GPT, a workflow automation, a content system, or a research assistant.

sections.content: Choose one: Custom GPT (shareable persona), Workflow Automation (repeating task), or Content System (writes in your voice). Pick based on pain.
Write your brief before touching anything: "A [type] for [audience] that [function]." Two minutes of clarity saves hours of rework.
Pick a project you actually need. Real stakes drive you through the friction. Scope small: complete one full cycle (brief → build → test → deploy). Define done before you start.
Four project types. Brief first: a [type] for [audience] that [function]. Pick something you need. Scope small. Define done before building.

exercises.prompt: Which project brief gives ChatGPT the best starting point in GPT Builder?
options: Make me something useful.
A Custom GPT for my freelance photography clients that shows my portfolio highlights, answers pricing questions, and helps them book a consultation — friendly and professional tone.
Build me a GPT with all the advanced features, integrations, and best-in-class UX.
I want the best GPT ever. Make it go viral.
exercises.explanation: Option B gives GPT Builder what it needs: what the project is (Custom GPT), who it's for (photography clients), what it does (shows portfolio, answers pricing, books consultations), and the tone (friendly and professional). Option A is too vague. C over-specifies features without describing purpose. D gives no actionable direction.
hints: The best brief describes what, who, what it does, and the feeling -- not the technical features.

exercises.prompt: You gave ChatGPT a vague brief ('I want a GPT for my business') and got generic questions back. Write a specific brief that gives ChatGPT everything it needs to start building.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You told ChatGPT 'I want a GPT for my business' and ChatGPT asked for more detail. Write the follow-up that gives ChatGPT everything: what you do, who your audience is, what the GPT needs to handle, the tone, and the core function.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["service","business","do","offer","sell","provide"],"label":"Described the business","points":1},{"keyword_group":["client","customer","audience","visitor","user","for"],"label":"Defined the audience","points":1},{"keyword_group":["question","handle","answer","help","task","topic"],"label":"Listed what GPT should handle","points":2},{"keyword_group":["tone","feel","style","friendly","professional","warm"],"label":"Described the tone","points":1},{"keyword_group":["book","schedule","buy","sign up","contact","action","goal"],"label":"Specified the core goal","points":2}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"I run a dog walking service in Austin. My clients are busy professionals. The GPT should answer questions about my services, pricing, and availability, and help clients book their first walk. Tone: friendly, trustworthy, professional. Its main goal is getting people to book. Upload: my service menu and FAQ.","tips":["What you do + who it's for + what it handles + the tone + the goal = a buildable brief.","Mentioning what to upload gives the GPT actual knowledge to work from.","Specifying the primary goal (book a walk) gives the GPT a clear success metric."]}
exercises.explanation: A specific brief answers: what is it, who is it for, what does it handle, how should it feel, and what's the goal. Specificity turns generic output into something tailored to your actual business.
hints: Give ChatGPT the business context and purpose, not just tone preferences.

exercises.prompt: Walk through refining a vague project idea into something you can actually build.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "From Vague to Buildable", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You want to build something but your initial idea is vague: 'I want a GPT for my freelance work.' Let's refine it step by step."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Starting point: 'I want a GPT for my freelance work.' This could mean anything. What's the first thing to clarify?", "question": "What's the most important detail to add first?", "options": ["The color scheme of the interface", "What kind of freelance work and who hires you", "Which ChatGPT plan to use", "How many capabilities to include"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "What you do and who your audience is drives every other decision. A freelance photographer for wedding couples needs a completely different GPT than a freelance developer for startups."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Updated brief: 'I'm a freelance UX designer. My clients are startup founders looking for product design help.' Better. What's next?", "question": "The brief has what and who. What's missing?", "options": ["Nothing -- this is enough to start building", "What the GPT should do and what knowledge it needs to do it well", "A list of competitor GPTs to copy"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Startup founders evaluating a UX designer want to understand your process, see case studies, and figure out how to work with you. Defining what the GPT handles (and what files to upload) tells you exactly what to build."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "Now write the complete brief. You're a freelance UX designer targeting startup founders. Include what, who, what the GPT handles, tone, and the primary goal.", "placeholder": "Write your complete brief...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["UX", "design", "freelance", "product"], "label": "Stated the business", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["startup", "founder", "client", "hire"], "label": "Named the audience", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["portfolio", "case study", "work", "process"], "label": "Included portfolio/process topics", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["contact", "hire", "book", "call", "reach out"], "label": "Defined a goal action", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["tone", "professional", "approachable", "style", "voice"], "label": "Described the tone", "points": 1}], "ideal": "I'm a freelance UX designer targeting startup founders who need product design help. The GPT should explain my design process, share case study highlights, answer questions about working with me, and guide founders toward booking a discovery call. Tone: professional and approachable. Upload: portfolio PDF and process doc.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: A buildable brief refines through layers: what you do, who it's for, what it handles, tone, goal, and knowledge to upload.
hints: Build the brief in layers: business, audience, what it handles, tone, goal, files to upload.

lessons.title: Build a Custom GPT
lessons.description: Open GPT Builder, configure instructions and knowledge, test with real prompts, and publish.

sections.content: Open GPT Builder: Explore GPTs → Create. Set Name, Instructions (system prompt), Knowledge (files), Capabilities, and Actions. One section at a time.
Test before publishing: send a common question, a knowledge-file question, an edge case, a leading question, and "what can you help me with?" Fix every off-brand response.
Click Publish → Anyone with the link. Use it yourself for a week before sharing. Every GPT Builder edit you save applies instantly to all future conversations.

GPT Builder: Name, Instructions, Knowledge, Capabilities, Actions. Instructions are most important. Test five query types before publishing. Start with "Anyone with the link." Updates apply instantly.

exercises.prompt: You just opened GPT Builder for the first time. It asks you to describe your GPT. What do you say?
options: Tell it to be helpful and professional.
Use your one-sentence brief: 'A friendly assistant for my dog walking service that answers questions about services and helps clients book walks.'
Skip the guided setup and go straight to the instructions editor.
Ask GPT Builder what you should build.
exercises.explanation: Your one-sentence brief is exactly what GPT Builder needs to kickstart the configuration. It gives GPT Builder the context to suggest a starting set of instructions that you then refine — much faster than starting from a blank instructions editor.
hints: You wrote a one-sentence brief for exactly this moment.

exercises.prompt: Write the instructions for a Custom GPT that helps freelance UX designers respond to client inquiries. It should know your process, your rates, and how to guide prospects to a discovery call.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're building a Custom GPT for your UX design freelance practice. Prospective clients will use it to learn about your services. Write instructions that define behavior, tone, and what to do at every stage of a client inquiry.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["role","you are","assistant","I am","persona"],"label":"Defined the GPT's persona","points":1},{"keyword_group":["client","prospective","audience","founder","startup"],"label":"Named the audience","points":1},{"keyword_group":["process","how I work","approach","design","phases"],"label":"Referenced process/how you work","points":2},{"keyword_group":["discovery call","book","next step","schedule","conversation"],"label":"Guided to discovery call","points":2},{"keyword_group":["never","don't","avoid","always","constraint"],"label":"Included constraints","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"You are a friendly assistant for [Name]'s UX design practice. You help prospective clients understand how I work, what I charge, and whether we're a good fit. When asked about process, reference the uploaded process guide. When asked about rates, describe the project-based pricing model from the rate card. Always guide interested prospects toward booking a free discovery call at [URL]. Never quote exact timelines without knowing project scope. If you don't know something, say so and offer to have me follow up directly. Tone: professional, approachable, direct.","tips":["Explicit instructions about when to use uploaded files improve response accuracy.","A specific conversion goal (discovery call) gives the GPT direction.","'Never' and 'always' constraints prevent the most common off-brand responses."]}
exercises.explanation: Good instructions define persona, audience, when to use uploaded knowledge, the conversion goal, constraints, and tone. Each element shapes a different aspect of the GPT's behavior.
hints: Answer all seven: who it is, who it talks to, what it always does, what it never does, how it sounds, when to use files, what to do when it doesn't know.

exercises.prompt: Your Custom GPT is producing inconsistent responses. Walk through diagnosing and fixing the three most common problems.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Diagnosing and Fixing Your Custom GPT", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "Your dog walking Custom GPT is live but producing inconsistent results: sometimes it quotes wrong prices, sometimes it gives very long answers, and occasionally it recommends competitor services."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The GPT is quoting wrong prices. It doesn't seem to be using the uploaded price list consistently.", "question": "What's the most likely fix?", "options": ["Re-upload the price list", "Add explicit instructions: 'When asked about pricing, always reference the uploaded service menu. Never quote rates from memory.'", "Remove the price list and just include pricing in the instructions"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "GPT needs to be explicitly told when to use uploaded files. 'When asked about pricing, always reference the uploaded service menu' solves this. Re-uploading doesn't help if the GPT isn't instructed to use the file."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Responses are too long -- clients are getting walls of text when they just want a quick answer.", "question": "How do you fix verbose responses?", "options": ["Ask users to say 'be brief' in their first message", "Add length guidance to instructions: 'Keep responses under 150 words unless asked for more. Lead with the direct answer, then offer to elaborate.'", "Switch to a shorter knowledge file"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Length guidance in instructions is the right fix. It applies consistently to all responses. Asking users to manage length is friction; fixing it in the instructions removes that burden."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "The GPT occasionally mentions competitor services. Write the instruction that prevents this.", "placeholder": "Write your constraint instruction...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["never", "don't", "avoid", "not discuss", "only"], "label": "Used a clear constraint word", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["competitor", "other service", "alternative", "other company"], "label": "Named what to avoid", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["instead", "redirect", "focus on", "our service", "help with"], "label": "Provided the alternative behavior", "points": 2}], "ideal": "Never mention, recommend, or discuss competitor services. If a user asks about alternatives, politely explain what [Business Name] offers and how it meets their needs instead.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: The three most common Custom GPT problems: not using uploaded files (fix with explicit file-use instructions), verbose responses (fix with length guidance), and off-brand topics (fix with explicit constraints).
hints: Each problem has a specific fix: file-use instructions, length guidance, or explicit constraints.

lessons.title: Build a Workflow Automation
lessons.description: Map a repetitive task as trigger-process-output, build a master prompt, test with real data, make it reusable.

sections.content: Map as trigger → process → output before building. Best candidates: you can explain the steps in 2 minutes and run them at least weekly.
Build one master prompt: "When I paste [input], run: Step 1 [task], Step 2 [task]. Output [format]." Test with real data. Fix the prompt, not just the output.
When a master prompt is stable for 3-4 weeks, promote it to a Custom GPT: paste into Builder, upload reference files, add a conversation starter.
Map as trigger-process-output. Build one master prompt, test with real data, fix the prompt. Save permanently. Promote to Custom GPT when stable. Add steps for new requirements — don't rebuild.

exercises.prompt: Which is the better way to describe your automation target?
options: 'I want to automate some of my work tasks.'
'Every Monday, I compile a client status report by pulling updates from our project tracker, CRM notes, and email threads. It takes 45 minutes and the format is the same every week.'
'I need AI to handle my busywork.'
'I want to automate everything I do at work.'
exercises.explanation: Option B is specific: when (Monday), what (status report), where data comes from (3 sources), how long (45 min), and the key insight (same format weekly). This gives enough detail to actually design the automation.
hints: Which description gives enough detail to actually start building?

exercises.prompt: Write a master prompt that chains three workflow steps into a single ChatGPT command: parse raw client data, write per-client summaries, and format into a weekly report template.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You have three tested steps: parse raw client data, write summaries per client, and format into your report template. Write a single master prompt that chains all three and specifies the trigger, steps, and output requirements.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["paste","data","below","input","raw","when"],"label":"Defined the trigger","points":1},{"keyword_group":["parse","extract","status","revenue","blocker"],"label":"Step 1: Data parsing","points":2},{"keyword_group":["write","summary","sentence","highlight","win"],"label":"Step 2: Summary writing","points":1},{"keyword_group":["format","template","report","date","section"],"label":"Step 3: Formatting","points":1},{"keyword_group":["flag","down","risk","attention","10%"],"label":"Added flagging logic","points":2}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"When I paste this week's raw client data below, run the following workflow end to end: Step 1: Parse the raw data and extract status, revenue, and blockers for each client. Step 2: Write a 2-3 sentence summary for each client highlighting wins and flagging risks. Step 3: Format everything into the weekly report structure: date header, executive summary (3 sentences: biggest win, biggest risk, outlook), then per-client sections. Output the final report ready to copy into an email. Flag any client with revenue down more than 10% from last week.","tips":["Clear trigger, sequenced steps, specific output requirements.","The executive summary constraint (3 sentences) prevents the 'too long' problem.","Flagging anomalies (revenue down 10%+) adds intelligence beyond formatting."]}
exercises.explanation: A master prompt: clear trigger (paste data), sequenced steps (parse, write, format), specific output (ready to email), and intelligence (flag anomalies). This single prompt replaces the multi-step manual process.
hints: Trigger first, steps in order, output requirements last.

exercises.prompt: Your master prompt works but requirements just changed. Walk through adapting the system without rebuilding.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Adapting a Living System", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "Your weekly report automation has been running perfectly for 4 weeks. Your boss just said: 'Starting next week, I also need a competitive comparison section. Pull from the market research doc I can share.'"}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "New requirement: add a competitive comparison section using market research data.", "question": "How do you handle this change?", "options": ["Rebuild the entire automation from scratch with the new section", "Add the new section as Step 4 in the existing master prompt and upload the market research doc to the Custom GPT", "Tell your boss the automation can't handle new requirements"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Add a step to the existing prompt, don't rebuild. The other sections still work. Upload the market research doc to the Custom GPT so it's always available. 10-minute update to a system that took 2 hours to build."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You added Step 4 and the competitive section looks good. But the executive summary didn't automatically incorporate competitive insights.", "question": "The executive summary doesn't reflect the new section. How do you fix it?", "options": ["Manually add competitive insights to the summary each week", "Update the executive summary instruction: 'Executive summary: 3 sentences -- biggest client win, biggest competitive shift, outlook for next week'", "Remove the executive summary"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Update the instruction to include competitive insights. One-line change to the prompt, permanently fixed."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "The system now has 4 steps and the exec summary includes competitive insights. Write the updated master prompt with all sections.", "placeholder": "Write the updated master prompt...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["step 1", "parse", "extract", "data"], "label": "Kept original parsing step", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["step 2", "summary", "write", "client"], "label": "Kept original summary step", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["competitive", "comparison", "market", "research"], "label": "Added competitive section", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["executive", "3 sentence", "win", "competitive shift"], "label": "Updated exec summary to include competitive", "points": 2}], "ideal": "Updated master prompt: Step 1) Parse raw data -- extract status, revenue, blockers per client. Step 2) Write 2-3 sentence summaries per client. Step 3) Compare each client's metrics against competitive landscape from the market research doc. Step 4) Format into report structure. Executive summary: 3 sentences -- biggest client win, biggest competitive shift, outlook. Flag revenue down 10%+. Output ready to email.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Living systems adapt through incremental updates, not rebuilds. When requirements change, add a step to the existing prompt, update affected sections, and test.
hints: What's the minimum change to add the new requirement? Don't rebuild what works.

lessons.title: Build a Content System
lessons.description: Design a voice-consistent content system using a Custom GPT: configure your voice, build topic queues, and produce on-brand content at scale.

sections.content: A content system is a Custom GPT with four pillars: voice doc, audience context, topic queue, and format templates.
Write your voice doc from 3-5 of your best pieces. Turn observations into explicit rules: not "professional tone" but "first-person, under 20 words per sentence, no em dashes."
Topic queue format: Topic, Angle, Audience, Platform, Status. Tell the GPT: "Select next unpublished item for this platform, confirm before drafting." One trigger prompt → draft ready to review.
Content system = Custom GPT with voice doc, audience context, topic queue, and format templates. Topic queue → one prompt → draft. Scale by adding formats.

exercises.prompt: Which is the best first project for a content system?
options: An all-platform content machine that handles LinkedIn, Twitter, YouTube, newsletters, and podcasts at launch
A focused LinkedIn content system that produces 2-3 posts per week in your voice, starting with voice calibration
A fully automated system that publishes content without any human review
A viral content generator that optimizes for maximum engagement
exercises.explanation: Start focused: one platform, a realistic publishing cadence, and voice calibration as the first priority. All-platform from day one means no platform is done well. Automated publishing without review is a path to off-brand content going public. Optimizing for virality before establishing your voice produces content that doesn't sound like you.
hints: What's the minimum viable content system you'd actually use consistently?

exercises.prompt: You want to train your content system Custom GPT on your LinkedIn writing style. Write a prompt that gives ChatGPT everything it needs to analyze your voice from three sample posts.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're pasting three of your best LinkedIn posts to build your voice document. Write the analysis prompt that will produce a voice guide ChatGPT can follow.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["analyze","observe","study","identify","look at"],"label":"Asked for analysis","points":1},{"keyword_group":["sentence","length","structure","pattern","opening"],"label":"Referenced structural patterns","points":2},{"keyword_group":["avoid","don't","never","what I don't","phrases I don't"],"label":"Asked for what to avoid","points":2},{"keyword_group":["document","guide","rules","write up","produce"],"label":"Asked for a usable output document","points":1},{"keyword_group":["tone","voice","style","sound like","my writing"],"label":"Focused on voice","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Analyze these three LinkedIn posts and produce a voice guide I can load into my content system. Identify: my typical sentence length, opening patterns, structural patterns (bullets, numbered lists, paragraphs), words and phrases I use frequently, and words/phrases I clearly avoid. Output a voice document written as explicit rules (e.g., 'Always use first-person. Keep sentences under 20 words. Never use em dashes'). Make it specific enough that someone could produce content indistinguishable from mine by following these rules.","tips":["Asking for explicit rules (not descriptions) produces a document ChatGPT can follow.","'Indistinguishable from mine' sets the right quality bar.","Including what to avoid is as important as what to include."]}
exercises.explanation: A voice analysis prompt that produces a usable voice document: asks for pattern analysis, structural observations, words to use and avoid, and outputs explicit rules — not a description.
hints: The output should be a set of rules, not a description of your style.

exercises.prompt: Your content system Custom GPT is built. Walk through the workflow from topic idea to published post.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Topic to Published Post", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "Your content system Custom GPT has your voice guide, audience context, topic queue, and LinkedIn format template uploaded. You just had a conversation with a client that sparked a great post idea."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You just had a great conversation with a client about why most AI implementations fail. You want to turn this into a LinkedIn post.", "question": "What's your first step?", "options": ["Open ChatGPT and start writing immediately while it's fresh", "Add the idea to your topic queue document first, then ask the content system to draft from it", "Post your raw notes to LinkedIn"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Add to the topic queue first -- even when inspiration is fresh. This keeps your queue current and lets the content system draft from the structured entry rather than raw notes. Then draft."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You've added the topic to the queue: 'Why most AI implementations fail -- most companies buy tools before building the habit of actually using AI. Audience: senior leaders evaluating AI investments.'", "question": "How do you trigger the draft?", "options": ["Open a regular ChatGPT conversation and explain the topic from scratch", "Open your content system Custom GPT and say 'Draft the next LinkedIn post from my topic queue'", "Ask ChatGPT to write a generic AI leadership post"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "The content system has everything it needs: your voice, your audience, your format, and the topic queue. One sentence kicks off the draft. That's the whole value of having the system built."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "The draft came back. It's 80% right -- the voice sounds like you and the structure is good, but the opening hook is too generic. Write your feedback prompt.", "placeholder": "Write your revision prompt...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["opening", "hook", "first", "lead", "beginning"], "label": "Named the specific element", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["generic", "too broad", "not specific", "cliché", "bland"], "label": "Described the problem", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["specific", "story", "example", "concrete", "real"], "label": "Suggested the direction", "points": 2}], "ideal": "The opening hook is too generic -- it could be from any AI thought leader. Replace it with something specific: a concrete moment from the client conversation that illustrates the problem directly. Lead with the specific, not the general.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Topic to post: add to queue, trigger from the content system, give specific feedback on what's off and in what direction. The system handles the heavy lifting; you handle the direction and final judgment.
hints: The content system does the work; you give direction and approve.

lessons.title: Ship and Iterate
lessons.description: Deploy your Custom GPT, lock in your reusable workflow, share with your team, and set up for continuous improvement.

sections.content: Ship it. Custom GPTs: Publish → Anyone with the link. Workflow automations: run on real data. Content systems: use for three real posts. Use it yourself first.
Your project isn't frozen — edit in GPT Builder anytime. The real skill you built: brief → configure → iterate → ship. That pattern works on every project.
To hand off: share the link and write one paragraph — what to paste, what the output looks like, who to contact. Ask for feedback after first real use.
Publish Custom GPTs with "Anyone with the link." Edit in GPT Builder — updates apply instantly. Hand off with a one-paragraph guide. Ask for real-user feedback. Demonstrated beats described.

exercises.prompt: You want to add a new capability to your published Custom GPT next week. What's the right approach?
options: Create a brand new Custom GPT from scratch with the added capability.
Open GPT Builder, edit the instructions to add the new capability, upload any new knowledge files, and save -- the update applies immediately.
Tell users to describe what they want in each conversation since the GPT can't be changed.
Wait until you have enough changes to justify a complete rebuild.
exercises.explanation: Edit in GPT Builder and save. The update applies immediately to all future conversations. Custom GPTs are living tools -- every edit is an improvement, not a rebuild.
hints: The workflow pattern doesn't change for small updates.

exercises.prompt: A user tested your Custom GPT and found it gives overly long answers and sometimes goes off-topic into areas the GPT wasn't designed for. Write the instruction updates that fix both problems.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"Real-user feedback revealed two problems: responses are too long, and the GPT occasionally answers questions outside its scope. Write the instruction additions that fix both issues.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["length","word","short","concise","brief","under"],"label":"Added length constraint","points":2},{"keyword_group":["unless","unless asked","if asked for more","detail on request"],"label":"Allowed elaboration on request","points":1},{"keyword_group":["only","scope","don't","never","outside","topic"],"label":"Added scope constraint","points":2},{"keyword_group":["redirect","instead","what you can help with","politely"],"label":"Defined redirect behavior","points":2}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Keep all responses under 150 words unless the user explicitly asks for more detail. Lead with the direct answer. For questions outside your scope (anything beyond [Business Name]'s services, pricing, and booking), politely redirect: 'I'm focused on helping with [specific topics]. For other questions, you might try [general suggestion or direct them to contact].' Never attempt to answer off-topic questions comprehensively.","tips":["Specific word count (150) is more reliable than 'be concise.'","'Unless asked for more' keeps you helpful for users who want depth.","A specific redirect response prevents the GPT from going silent on off-topic questions."]}
exercises.explanation: Two common problems, two targeted fixes: length guidance with an 'unless asked' escape valve, and a scope constraint with a specific redirect behavior. Both are instruction-level fixes.
hints: Fix each problem with a specific instruction. Vague guidance produces vague improvement.

exercises.prompt: Your colleague wants to use the workflow automation Custom GPT you built. Walk through the handoff.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "The Team Handoff", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You built a meeting notes → action items + summary Custom GPT that saves you 30 minutes per meeting. Your colleague wants to use it too. They're not technical and have never used a Custom GPT before."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Your colleague asks: 'How do I use this?' What's the right handoff?", "question": "What do you share?", "options": ["Explain how you built the GPT and configured the instructions", "Share the link and a one-paragraph guide: what to paste in, what it produces, and what to do if something looks wrong", "Tell them to read the GPT's system prompt for context"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "User-level handoff: the link + a simple guide. They don't need to know how it works. They need to know how to use it."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "After one week, your colleague reports: 'It mostly works but sometimes the action items are wrong when the notes mention different projects in the same meeting.'", "question": "How do you handle this feedback?", "options": ["Tell them to split multi-project meetings into separate notes before pasting", "Update the GPT instructions: 'When notes reference multiple projects, clearly label each action item with its project name. Group action items by project.'", "Create a separate Custom GPT for multi-project meetings"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Update the instructions -- that fixes it for all future users, not just this one. Asking users to pre-process input is friction. Separate GPTs for edge cases adds complexity without adding value."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "You've collected feedback from three teammates. Write the one-paragraph handoff guide for the updated GPT.", "placeholder": "Write your handoff guide...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["paste", "copy", "input", "how to use", "to start"], "label": "Explained how to trigger", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["produces", "output", "generates", "you get"], "label": "Described the output", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["contact", "tell me", "let me know", "something wrong", "issue"], "label": "Provided escalation path", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["project", "label", "multiple", "group"], "label": "Mentioned multi-project behavior", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Open the Meeting Notes GPT and paste your raw meeting notes. It will produce: labeled action items grouped by project, a 3-sentence summary, and a follow-up draft. For meetings covering multiple projects, it automatically groups items. If anything looks wrong or the output seems off, let me know and I'll update the instructions.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Team handoff: share the link, write a user-level guide (what to paste, what you get, who to contact). Turn feedback into instruction updates, not user instructions. The tool should handle edge cases, not the users.
hints: The guide is for the user, not the builder. Keep it at their level.

lessons.title: What You Built and What's Next
lessons.description: Reflect on what you built, extract the transferable lessons, and plan your next project.

sections.content: Before your next project: 15 minutes on three questions — what worked, what took longer, what to add to the brief. Those answers are your template for the next build.
Best second project: different category than your first. Build range: one Custom GPT, one workflow, one content system. Search the GPT Store before building.
Advanced capabilities once you've shipped three projects: Custom Actions (connect GPTs to external APIs), Code Interpreter (analyze spreadsheets), DALL-E (generate images), multi-GPT chaining.
Retrospect: what worked, what took longer, what to add to the brief. Build range across categories. Search the GPT Store first. Monthly maintenance keeps your stack current.

exercises.prompt: You want to add a new capability to your live Custom GPT that users have been requesting. What's the right approach?
options: Create a brand new Custom GPT with the new capability and deprecate the old one.
Open GPT Builder, add the new capability to the instructions and knowledge, save -- the update applies immediately to all future conversations.
Tell users to describe the new capability themselves in each conversation.
Wait until you have 10 feature requests before making any changes.
exercises.explanation: Edit in GPT Builder and save. The update applies immediately. Custom GPTs are living tools designed to evolve. Rebuilding creates discontinuity. Asking users to work around missing capabilities is unnecessary friction.
hints: Custom GPTs are editable at any time. Updates apply immediately.

exercises.prompt: You're planning your second ChatGPT project. Write a one-sentence brief for a project in a different category than your first, and explain why you chose it.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"Your first project was a Custom GPT for client inquiries. You're now planning a second project in a different category. Write a one-sentence brief for a workflow automation or content system, and explain your reasoning.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["workflow","automation","content system","research assistant"],"label":"Chose a different category","points":2},{"keyword_group":["for","audience","client","customer","team","my"],"label":"Named the audience","points":1},{"keyword_group":["that","which","to","by","produces","handles"],"label":"Described the function","points":2},{"keyword_group":["because","reason","why","saves","need","problem"],"label":"Explained the reasoning","points":2}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"A weekly workflow automation for my freelance practice that takes raw project notes and produces a client-ready status update in my voice, ready to email -- because writing these updates currently takes 30 minutes each and I do five of them per week.","tips":["The brief should pass the 'would I recognize this when built' test.","The reasoning reveals whether this solves a real problem.","Frequency and time cost justify why this is worth building next."]}
exercises.explanation: A good project brief for the second project: different category, specific audience, clear function, and reasoning grounded in a real time-cost problem.
hints: The best second project solves your most frequent pain point after the first one.

exercises.prompt: Six months from now: you have a Custom GPT, a workflow automation, and a content system all running. Walk through the monthly maintenance check that keeps them current.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Monthly Maintenance Check", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "It's been a month since you last reviewed your three projects. You have: a client inquiry Custom GPT, a weekly report workflow automation, and a LinkedIn content system. 20 minutes total for all three."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You open the client inquiry Custom GPT first. You notice it references a pricing tier you discontinued last month.", "question": "What do you do?", "options": ["Leave it for now -- not many people ask about that tier", "Update the uploaded service menu with current pricing, save the updated file to the GPT's knowledge section", "Create a new GPT with current pricing"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Update the file in the knowledge section. It takes 5 minutes and fixes the accuracy for all future conversations. Leaving stale pricing is a credibility risk. Rebuilding is unnecessary."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The weekly report workflow has been producing good output, but your team now uses a different project tracker that has different status categories.", "question": "How do you handle the changed input format?", "options": ["Tell the team to manually reformat data before pasting", "Update the master prompt Step 1: add the new status categories to the parsing instructions", "Build a separate workflow for the new tracker"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Update Step 1 of the master prompt to include the new categories. The team doesn't manage input format -- you manage the system. A one-line update to the parsing step handles this."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "For the content system, write a quick self-audit prompt that checks if the voice guide still reflects how you write today.", "placeholder": "Write your voice audit prompt...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["compare", "check", "review", "match", "still"], "label": "Asked for comparison", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["recent", "new", "latest", "last month", "current"], "label": "Referenced current writing", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["voice", "style", "tone", "sound like", "writing"], "label": "Focused on voice", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["update", "change", "gap", "different", "revise"], "label": "Asked for gaps/changes", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Here are three pieces I wrote last month [paste]. Compare them to the voice guide in my content system. What has changed or drifted? Are there new patterns in my recent writing that the voice guide doesn't capture? Suggest specific updates to keep the guide current.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Monthly maintenance: update knowledge files when facts change, update workflow prompts when input formats change, audit voice guides against recent writing. 20 minutes per month keeps all three projects accurate and useful.
hints: Each project type has a different maintenance focus: facts (Custom GPT), input format (workflow), voice drift (content system).
